Shabbat is there and we're noting its arrival. Right? The Shabbat Malchuta is arriving and we're acknowledging its presence. We're not doing anything in order to bring the Shabbat in really. So the rabbis all wonder what's going on over here. We have a few different approaches to deal with this question. The Ibn Ezra talks about Al-Piyapshat. This idea of what it means to La'asot et HaShabbat. To make the Shabbat. The Ibn Ezra says, Sheikh taken serachav udrachav bayom ha'shishi, kedeshi yishmor ha'Shabbat ve'lo yichalelena. It says in the Ibn Ezra that we have an obligation of La'asot et HaShabbat that kicks in on the weekday, on Friday. That a person has to do everything he can in order to prepare for Shabbat correctly. And when he does some of that work, when he invests a little bit into the weekday, into his Erev Shabbat, when he turns Friday not into a mundane day, but Shabbat is Erev Shabbat. It's a spiritual entity in itself in order to prepare you for the Shabbat itself. When you put the work in over there, you'll be able to create the best Shabbat experience for yourself. If a person just arrives at Shabbat, if he doesn't do anything during the weekday, especially on Friday, to put himself both physically in terms of food, in terms of what he has to prepare, because he can't do any work on Shabbat, but also spiritually to turn his Friday afternoon into something holy, turn his Friday afternoon into a time of a little bit of anticipation for the Shabbat. When you do that to yourself, if you go take a hot shower on Erev Shabbat and you have in mind, I'm doing this for Shabbat, I'm doing this in order to prepare for the arrival of the Shabbat queen, a person puts himself in that right frame of mind and also physically he does all of his actions. I'm cooking the roast on Friday afternoon, that's la'asot et ha'Shabbat, I'm doing actions that'll help me deal with v'shameru later on. The la'asot that I do on Friday afternoon will help me avoid doing any yisur melechah, any prohibition when Shabbat eventually arrives. That's the pshat (simple explanation) that the Ibn Ezra offers. The Orachaim HaKadosh brings down another perush to this and he says that we see this word shamar elsewhere. We go to Sefer Bereshit, back to Yaakov Avinu, where Yosef had all these dreams and Yosef had these different ideas of what was going to be and the pasuk says ve'aviv shamar et ha'davar. His father, Yaakov Avinu, after watching the interactions between Yosef and his brothers, he noted what was going on and says the Orachaim over here that he was mamtin - waiting. It didn't just mean he noted it in his head, but he was waiting with anticipation when is this going to happen. He was on guard and he was very aware of the situation and anticipating its arrival so he would deal with it properly between the interaction between the brothers and Yosef. Ve'aviv shamar et ha'davar. Yaakov Avinu noted what happened but he was anticipating the next steps in order to deal with it properly. And that's what the Orachaim says over here that when we wait with Shabbat we have different ways to relate to Shabbat. On a very simple level and we all enjoy it very much, Shabbat could be a time of relaxation. We have a very busy week. We're tired, especially in a time where we're stimulated with technology all day and we don't have a moment to just kick back and relax and to just disconnect from everything going on around us. Whether it's work, whether it's helping in different institutions, whatever we're doing, Shabbat is a time that we have to just disconnect and relax. It's a little bit of a respite from all the craziness of the week. It's looked at as an off day. Says the Orachaim, that's one level to Shabbat. That's Shabbat menuha. But the higher level of Shabbat is la'asot et ha'Shabbat. To recognize that it's a day of asiyah. It's a day of opportunity as opposed to simply a day of relaxation. The whole idea of the relaxation that Shabbat offers us is to get away from the mundane and to get away from all that craziness but not to just chill. But in order to say, okay, now I have this vacuum of time that's open, that's available, that I can take and I can devote now to spirituality. We live our life in a way where the busy world we live in with no stop sometimes distracts us from the bigger purpose of what we're doing over here. The bigger purpose of what a Jew has to do is to try and attain in his life a certain measure of holiness, a certain measure of kedushah. During the week we have sparks of that here and there. I come to shul in the morning, I learn a little Torah, I go to, I pray, I this, I that, I hear the Torah on the way. But most of our day is filled with having to take care of ourselves, our family, running around, taking care of errands. We're busy. Shabbat is a time where we say, phone's away. I'm not going to work. I'm not driving anywhere. I'm going to relax. I'm going to have a good dinner. But the ultimate goal there is not just a off day and a day to chill, but it's okay. Now how do I maximize this availability that I have? Am I setting time aside to learn a little Torah? Am I reviewing the parashah correctly? Am I doing shenayim mikra ve'ahad targum? Am I spending time talking over Torah with my family? How am I treating that time? What am I doing with that opportunity that Hashem sends our way? So it says, the Or HaChaim HaKadosh, that's what it means, la'asot et haShabbat. V'shamiru b'nei Yisrael et haShabbat. When I'm guarding and treating the Shabbat properly, which is me staying away from everything I'm not allowed to do, then I should be la'asot et haShabbat. Then I have to make the Shabbat. I have to turn the Shabbat into a situation where it's not just going to be a chill day, but it's going to be a day that I take advantage of, a day that I maximize and use the time properly. So that's first the Ibn Ezra, and that's the Or HaChaim.
That's the pshat (simple explanation) that the Ibn Ezra offers.
